title: Intro
text: Welcome to Science Radar, your weekly digest of the most surprising, frontier-pushing, and cross-disciplinary discoveries from across all of science. This week we've found papers that challenge long-held assumptions, open doors to entirely new research directions, and connect ideas across fields in ways nobody expected. Let's dive in.

title: This Week's Highlights
text: And now, a quick tour through this week's other noteworthy papers.

title: A step-by-step guide to nailing your tenure promotion package
text:
A step-by-step guide to nailing your tenure promotion package — Nature News.

Nature, Published online: 20 April 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-00990-2

It’s an important milestone in many academic careers, yet the tenure process is surprisingly nebulous. Here’s what it takes to succeed.

title: The robust, high-throughput, and temporally regulated roxCre and loxCre reporting systems for genetic modifications in vivo
text: The robust, high-throughput, and temporally regulated roxCre and loxCre reporting systems for genetic modifications in vivo — eLife. Cre-loxP technology, a cornerstone in fate mapping and in vivo gene function studies, faces challenges in achieving precise and efficient conditional mutagenesis through inducible systems. This study introduces two innovative genetic tools designed to overcome these limitations. The first, roxCre, e

title: New idtracker.ai rethinks multi-animal tracking as a representation learning problem to increase accuracy and reduce tracking time
text: New idtracker.ai rethinks multi-animal tracking as a representation learning problem to increase accuracy and reduce tracking time — eLife. idTracker and idtracker.ai approach multi-animal tracking from video as an image classification problem. For this classification, both rely on segments of video where all animals are visible to extract images and their identity labels. When these segments are too short, tracking can become slow and

title: Immune cells have a surprising role in exercise endurance
text:
Immune cells have a surprising role in exercise endurance — Nature News.

Nature, Published online: 17 April 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-01245-w

Study in mice suggests that B cells help to regulate muscle performance.

title: How individual vigor shapes human–human physical interaction
text: How individual vigor shapes human–human physical interaction — eLife. The speed of voluntary movements varies systematically, with some individuals moving consistently faster than others across different actions. These variations, conceptualized as vigor, reflect a time–effort–accuracy tradeoff in motor planning. How do two mechanically coupled partners with different

title: Dimorphic neural network architecture prioritizes sexual-related behaviors in male Caenorhabditis elegans
text: Dimorphic neural network architecture prioritizes sexual-related behaviors in male Caenorhabditis elegans — eLife. Neural network architecture determines its functional output. However, the detailed mechanisms are not well characterized. title: Outro
text: That's Science Radar for this week. Every one of these papers is a reminder that the most interesting science often lives at the edges — where fields collide, where assumptions break, and where the next big question is just starting to take shape. Until next week, keep questioning everything.
